Effective 1 December 2025, all occupational infectious diseases in healthcare, research, and laboratory settings, plus work-related musculoskeletal disorders, become reportable and compensable. Updated Workplace Safety and Health Guidelines now provide doctors with clearer guidance on detection, diagnosis, and treatment. Additionally, the reporting process for primary care doctors has been simplified to facilitate earlier detection and intervention. The Ministry continues collaborating with the Ministry of Health, NTUC, and industry partners through the Tripartite Committee on Workplace Health.

results.title: Progress in Implementing FWAs for Employees with Caregiving Responsibilities and Establishing System to Recognise Primary Caregivers
results.summary: A 2024 Ministry of Manpower survey shows 72.5% of private companies offer flexible work arrangements, up from 68.0% in 2023. The Government and tripartite partners launched the Tripartite Guidelines on FWA Requests in 2024 to formalize the process for employees to request arrangements and for employers to consider them. While law mandates paid maternity, paternity, childcare, and unpaid infant care leave, 6,100 companies (36.1%) voluntarily provided additional paid family care leave and 5,800 (34.0%) offered paid child sick leave in 2024. The Ministry encourages mutual agreement on arrangements rather than establishing a system to verify primary caregiver status, though it will continue reviewing available support.

results.title: Progress on Installation of EV Chargers in HDB Car Parks and Public Spaces
results.summary: Singapore is on track to deploy 60,000 EV charging points by 2030, with over 25,000 currently operational, nearly half of which are publicly accessible. For HDB car parks, more than 7,500 chargers are installed across approximately 85% of sites, with a target to provide convenient access to every resident by year-end. The Land Transport Authority monitors usage data to guide future deployments. Future scaling will focus on high-demand locations where electrical capacity permits, ensuring alignment with the latest charging technology and community needs.

results.title: Proposal to Publish Data on Commission Fees and Contractual Clauses of Delivery Platforms
results.summary: The Competition and Consumer Commission of Singapore (CCCS) does not routinely collect or publish commercially sensitive data, such as commission fees or contractual clauses, from e-commerce and food-delivery platforms. Instead, the agency monitors market developments and intervenes against anti-competitive practices, having conducted multiple investigations in the online food delivery sector since 2016 and recently intervening in a relevant merger. To address merchant treatment and market fairness, CCCS collaborated with Enterprise Singapore to enhance Technical Reference 76, building on its 2020 market study guidelines. The Government commits to investigating market failures and intervening when necessary, while welcoming feedback from businesses and consumers to improve enforcement effectiveness in the digital space.

results.title: Protocols and Training for Mosque Personnel to Handle and Report Suspicious Packages
results.summary: MUIS has established clear safety protocols for all mosque personnel, including staff and volunteers, to handle and report suspicious or hazardous packages. Upon identification of such items, personnel must contact the Police, support investigations, and ensure public safety while providing prompt updates to congregants. Regular training sessions are conducted to ensure all mosque personnel are familiar with these procedures. The Acting Minister-in-charge of Muslim Affairs confirmed these measures are in place following recent incidents involving meat parcels sent to local mosques.

results.title: Publishing Guidelines on Tackling Bullying in Schools
results.summary: The Ministry of Education operates within Singapore's existing legal framework to address bullying, utilizing student handbooks and parent sessions to communicate disciplinary rules. Upon establishing facts, schools impose consequences, educate students to restore relationships, and refer severe cases to the Police. For incidents involving complex family issues, schools support students while referring families to community agencies for appropriate assistance. The Minister confirmed that guidelines or regulations are available to schools but did not specify a separate publication timeline for the public. Schools engage parents to inform them of incident facts, co-create safety plans, and provide resources to support children at home.

results.title: Rationale for Plans to Ease Tax Framework Requirements for Single Family Offices
results.summary: The Monetary Authority of Singapore is reviewing tax schemes for Single Family Offices to reduce operational friction and ensure continued economic benefits. The review aims to streamline application documentation, simplify reporting obligations, and expand eligible investment types based on industry feedback. MAS reports no net outflow of Single Family Offices in the last three quarters, noting that these entities generate business revenue and jobs through external service providers. Furthermore, growing Single Family Office presence supports local communities via platforms like the Wealth Management Institute and philanthropic networks such as Philanthropy Asia Alliance, with specific examples including the Ishk Tolaram Foundation and James Dyson Foundation supporting education and skills training.

results.title: Reducing Necessity for Mass Evacuation of Passengers via Train Tracks during Incidents
results.summary: Evacuation onto train tracks is a last resort reserved for stalled trains that cannot be moved within a reasonable timeframe or during emergencies like fire or smoke. Newer trains on the North-South, East-West, and Jurong Region Lines feature batteries enabling movement to the nearest station without traction power, while rescue trains assist in moving stalled trains for station disembarkation. During incidents, operators follow safety protocols with special attention to commuters with mobility challenges, deploying trained officers and Singapore Civil Defence Force personnel for assistance. A Rail Reliability Taskforce formed on 19 September 2025 will investigate faster recovery methods to further reduce the necessity for track evacuations, ensuring service resumes only after all passengers are safely evacuated.

results.title: Reports of Abandoned Vapes in Transport Areas and Extension of Vape Disposal Bins to Non-restricted Areas at Transport Nodes
results.summary: Reports of abandoned e-vaporisers in transport areas have not increased. E-vaporiser disposal bins are located at sites offering QuitVape and rehabilitation programmes, or where devices are not easily accessible to others. Approximately 1,000 Public Transport Operator staff have received Health Sciences Authority training to conduct consistent, fair, and safe enforcement activities. The Ministry has not yet extended bins to non-restricted areas or developed new Standard Operating Procedures for confronting vape users, though these remain open considerations.

results.title: Review of Clemency Procedure to Provide Death Row Prisoners and Families Advance Information of Petition Outcomes
results.summary: The Ministry of Home Affairs completed a review of the clemency procedure to provide death row prisoners and their families advance notification of petition outcomes. Previously, execution date and clemency outcome notifications were simultaneous; now, if a timely clemency petition is received after all legal proceedings conclude, the prisoner receives the clemency outcome notification prior to the execution date notification if the petition is unsuccessful. This change applies to prisoners awaiting capital punishment who have exhausted all legal channels regarding their conviction and sentence.

results.title: Review of Jobseeker Support Scheme's Income Ceiling to Match Current 75th Percentile Gross Monthly Income Levels
results.summary: In 2024, the 75th percentile gross monthly income for full-time employed residents is approximately $8,100 excluding employer CPF contributions and $9,300 including them. The SkillsFuture Jobseeker Support scheme currently sets its qualifying income ceiling at $5,000 excluding employer CPF, a threshold slightly above the median income of about $4,900. The Ministry of Manpower excludes employer CPF contributions when determining this threshold to target lower- to middle-income workers facing financial constraints. While the scheme is still in its early stages, the Ministry will review its effectiveness in helping workers re-enter employment and assess the case for extending eligibility to higher-earning individuals.

results.title: Reviewing Policy Framework of PayNow Surcharge Rules to Ensure Consistency of Consumer Protection Measures
results.summary: The Association of Banks in Singapore (ABS) prohibits merchants from imposing surcharges on PayNow transactions, though Singapore Pools currently applies such fees. ABS is actively engaging Singapore Pools through relevant banks to resolve this inconsistency. The Government notes that over 300,000 businesses use PayNow with transaction fees waived for end-consumers, while minimal fees apply only for additional services like notifications. The Ministry has not yet confirmed a formal policy review but acknowledges the issue regarding consumer protection consistency.

results.title: Reviewing Temporary Permit (Exhibition) Scheme to Ensure Level Playing Field between Established Firms and Newly-registered Firms
results.summary: The Minister clarified that the Temporary Permit (Exhibition) Scheme (TPES) is narrowly scoped for seasonal manpower in the MICE industry, restricting workers to Malaysian nationals, installation/teardown tasks only, six whitelisted venues, and a maximum three-week duration. Consequently, the scheme does not create an uneven playing field between established and newly-registered firms, as all eligible entities can access it for specific seasonal needs. The Ministry will continue to monitor and review the scheme's implementation to ensure its ongoing relevance and effectiveness.

results.title: Salary Review of Allied Health Professionals and Key Findings
results.summary: The last salary review for allied health professionals (AHPs) in public healthcare institutions was implemented in July 2025, with monthly base salary increases of up to 7%. The Ministry of Health will continue to regularly monitor remuneration packages to ensure market competitiveness. The Minister did not provide specific key findings, recommendations, or details on follow-up actions taken since the review, as these were not explicitly stated in the response.

results.title: Singapore's Support of Newly Announced Gaza Peace Plan by US
results.summary: Singapore welcomed the 9 October 2025 agreement between Israel and Hamas on the first phase of the US-proposed Gaza peace plan, which is now being implemented. The government urges further progress on outstanding issues to enable Gaza's reconstruction and regional stability. Singapore will continue providing humanitarian assistance and supporting the Palestinian Authority through capacity building programmes. The Minister noted that creating conditions for reconstruction is vital for bringing hope to Gaza and enhancing Middle East stability.

results.title: Sovereign Limits on Foreign-sourced Military Equipment Used by SAF
results.summary: The Minister stated that Singapore procures both overseas and locally made defence equipment based on operational needs and cost-effectiveness. Acquisitions are subject to end-user obligations regarding proprietary technology and third-party transfers, which are assessed before purchase to ensure the SAF can deploy equipment while complying with conditions. In many cases, foreign equipment elements are localised through technology transfers and building local maintenance capabilities, providing added assurance. The response did not specify sovereign limits, independent action measures, or a timeline for reducing dependence on foreign-sourced equipment.

results.title: Standardising Bullying Incidents Management and Implementation across Schools
results.summary: The Ministry of Education defines bullying as persistent and intentional hurtful acts, distinguishing it from one-off insensitive comments or physical fights. Schools apply this understanding to manage various forms of bullying, including verbal, social, physical, and cyber incidents, using guidelines to formulate discipline policies. Disciplinary measures follow a tiered approach based on severity, ranging from reflection for careless remarks to detention, suspension, or caning for egregious cases. The Ministry provides these guidelines for schools to implement consistent management practices tailored to student profiles and needs.

results.title: Standardising School Dismissal Notification and Safety Procedures when Timing Deviates from Norm
results.summary: Primary schools standardise dismissal safety procedures by communicating standard timings and providing advance notice via Parents Gateway for any timing deviations. Students requiring early departure must obtain school permission, while those not picked up remain under supervised care until revised arrangements are confirmed. Schools ensure adequate safe spaces for pupils awaiting caregivers and require parents to inform the school if they cannot collect their child as scheduled.

results.title: Statistics for Fatalities and Injuries Related to MDWs Engaged in High-rise Cleaning
results.summary: Safety requirements for migrant domestic workers (MDWs) cleaning windows are prescribed under the Employment of Foreign Manpower (Work Passes) Regulations 2012, Fourth Schedule, mandating that exterior cleaning only occurs on grilles locked at all times with adult supervision. Employers breaching these rules face fines up to $10,000, imprisonment for up to 12 months, and potential bans from employing MDWs. Over the past decade, the Ministry of Manpower (MOM) took enforcement actions against six employers for negligence leading to falls and an average of 15 employers annually for safety standard non-compliance. All first-time employers and MDWs must attend mandatory orientation programmes covering safety practices and sign a safety agreement. MOM continues to monitor incidents, step up education and enforcement, and provides multilingual helplines for mental health support and rest-day advice.

results.title: Statistics on Private Property Purchases by Permanent Residents and Impact of Such Purchases on Housing Prices
results.summary: The Minister for National Development stated that Singapore Permanent Residents (PRs) account for a small, stable share of private residential property transactions, at 13% in the first three quarters of 2025 versus a five-year average of 15%. Consequently, PR purchases are not a key driver of private property price movements or housing affordability for citizens. The Ministry will continue to monitor the market closely and adjust policies as necessary to maintain a stable and sustainable property environment.

results.title: Studying Potential Productivity Gains from AI Adoption and Translating Gains into Wage Growth for Non-senior Managerial Workers
results.summary: The Government is studying the extent to which AI affects jobs, the labour market, and productivity, noting that isolating productivity gains and wage growth solely attributable to AI adoption is challenging due to rapidly evolving technology. The Minister stated that the full benefits of AI adoption will take time to materialise. Consequently, the Ministry will continue to study these impacts rather than providing immediate quantified projections on wage growth for non-senior managerial workers at this stage.

results.title: Subscription Numbers for Little Book Box Service and Plans to Improve Scheme
results.summary: In the October to December 2025 cycle, 870 subscribers signed up for the National Library Board's "Little Book Box" service, with 85% opting for books in both English and their selected Mother Tongue Language. The Minister reported that subscriptions follow a quarterly cycle lasting three months and noted the service has received positive feedback. No specific plans to improve the scheme were stated as the current figures appear to align with positive reception.

results.title: Support Provided to Swedish Car Maker for R&D Office in Singapore and Clawback Penalties Imposed following Closure Eight Months After Launch
results.summary: The Economic Development Board provides financial and non-financial support to companies meeting specific economic outcomes, including job creation, local employment, business spending, and fixed asset investment. Mechanisms exist to claw back subsidies from entities failing to meet these conditions. While the Government has imposed clawback provisions and penalties on a Swedish car maker that closed its R&D office eight months after launch, specific details regarding the support forms and penalty amounts are not disclosed due to commercial sensitivities and confidentiality requirements.

results.title: Support for Gifted Children Attending Classes at Local Universities ahead of Age Cohort
results.summary: The Ministry of Education supports exceptionally gifted students through personalised education plans that may include advanced classes, after-school programmes, and expert mentorship for specialised research at autonomous universities. Applications for early university admission are assessed individually to ensure students are academically and socio-emotionally ready. Existing provisions are tailored to specific abilities and needs rather than a universal scheme for all gifted children.

results.title: Support for Trade Associations to Attract, Retain and Develop Secretariat Talent
results.summary: The Government supports trade associations and chambers (TACs) through the Trade Association Committee Transformation Centre, which offers shared secretariat services for internationalisation, digitalisation, sustainability, and advocacy. Enterprise Singapore collaborates with the Singapore Business Federation to develop human capital via the TAC Fellowship Programme for Leadership Development, benefiting 60 professionals over three years, and the TAC Knowledge Series workshops, attended by over 210 members from more than 50 TACs since 2024. Additionally, the Government second officers to TACs to augment staff strength and enhance policy implementation. These measures aim to increase TAC capacity and capabilities to serve members and drive industry transformation projects effectively.

results.title: Support for Youth Athletes Juggling Sporting and Academic Commitments
results.summary: The Acting Minister for Culture, Community and Youth outlined the Ministry's strategy to support student-athletes balancing academic and sporting commitments through the Sport Excellence (spex) programmes. The High Performance Sport Institute (HPSI) partners with national sports associations to provide long-term planning, psychological support, and athlete life management. Exemptions from school activities and examinations are granted for national training and competitions, with ongoing collaboration between the Ministry of Culture, Community and Youth, SportSG, and the Ministry of Education. The Singapore Sports School (SSP) offers a dedicated pathway where education is wrapped around sports, allowing mid-stream transfers at the secondary level and facilitated entry for high-performing athletes post-secondary. To ensure career viability, the spexEducation Undergraduate Scholarship was introduced last year, and a Memorandum of Understanding signed in April this year between SSP and six Autonomous Universities aims to strengthen athlete-friendly practices and support for carded student-athletes.

results.title: Supporting Mental Well-being of Preschool Educators
results.summary: The Early Childhood Development Agency (ECDA) addresses preschool educator well-being through a relief staff pool for short-term manpower gaps and the Early Childhood Industry Digital Plan to fund digital solutions that streamline administrative workloads. ECDA also collaborates with training providers to offer Continuing Professional Development courses focused on building resilience and self-care strategies. Further details on these initiatives are available in the Minister's reply to a Parliamentary Question dated 2 July 2024.

results.title: Race and Religion
results.summary: The Government reaffirmed that Singapore is a secular, multiracial nation where race and religion must remain separate from politics to protect social harmony and democracy. Ministerial statements clarified that no promises were made to Noor Deros, and the Workers' Party's endorsement of him was rejected categorically alongside foreign interference from PAS, which crosses a red line requiring immediate disavowal. While concerns were raised regarding the timing of responses and the need for safe spaces for minority dialogue, the Government maintained that its current calibrated approach effectively manages these issues without compromising the foundational principles of unity, meritocracy, and the rule of law.

results.title: Tackling Driver Fatigue, and Workplace Fatalities and Injuries of SME Workers in Construction Sector
results.summary: The Ministry of Manpower will continue to engage employers to raise awareness of the Workplace Safety and Health Guidelines on Fatigue Management, which include practical guidance on providing rest breaks and managing shift work. The Ministry will also promote the adoption of vehicular safety technologies, such as cameras and sensors, to improve driver situational awareness and detect signs of fatigue or distraction. Regarding SMEs in the construction sector, MOM has stepped up inspections targeted at smaller-scale works throughout 2025, including solar panel installations, renovation projects, and small residential works during festive seasons. Companies with major injuries are required to engage appointed auditors to address gaps and prevent recurrence. Beyond enforcement, MOM will help SMEs build internal safety capabilities through the StartSAFE programme and greater adoption of technology solutions.

results.title: Tackling Risk of Agentic AI Capable of Autonomous Actions and Unforeseen Emergent Behaviours
results.summary: The Minister stated that existing legislation, including the Personal Data Protection Act and Broadcasting Act, alongside sector-specific guidelines, currently covers many AI risks. For agentic AI specifically, the Government applies existing frameworks emphasizing human accountability, oversight roles, and risk management processes while reviewing adaptations for increased autonomy. The Government is simultaneously running trials with the Government Technology Agency of Singapore to experiment with agentic AI in public sector use cases and understand responsible implementation. No specific new legislative empowerment to govern AI actions or generated content was announced at this time, though the Online Safety (Relief and Accountability) Bill is upcoming. The Government continues monitoring international developments to strengthen controls and guardrails for these emerging systems.

results.title: Take-up Rate for Hospital Clinician Scheme Across Specialties
results.summary: The Ministry of Health has reviewed the Hospital Clinician Scheme to enhance attractiveness, introducing a new apex grade with professional development opportunities comparable to specialist and consultant family physician tracks. Approximately 150 Hospital Clinicians are currently deployed across public healthcare institutions in specialties including medicine, surgery, paediatrics, and emergency medicine. These clinicians operate on a separate career track focused on broad-based competencies rather than specific specialties, serving as principal doctors within a new care team model. The Ministry is also reviewing remuneration to ensure parity with other tracks performing similar roles. Additionally, MOH collaborates with local medical schools to update students on career tracks through clinical rotations, guidance talks, and counselling, aiming to encourage consideration of all three public healthcare career paths.

results.title: Take-up Rate of Strategic Partnership Co-curricular Activity and Potential for Cluster-based Programme Development
results.summary: In 2025, 232 students from 86 schools participated in three Strategic Partnership Co-Curricular Activities (athletics, water polo, canoeing), an increase from 139 students in 2024. Hockey will be added next year, while current training venues are deemed adequate; additional locations will be explored if required. The Ministry encourages schools to initiate cluster-based CCAs to broaden activity access and foster friendships. MOE supports these partnerships to cater to diverse student strengths and interests, with joint efforts by MOE, MCCY, and Sport Singapore to facilitate expansion.

results.title: Target Contribution Payment Rates among Discharged Bankrupts from 2010 to 2020
results.summary: The Target Contribution concept was introduced in August 2016 under the Differentiated Discharge Framework (DDF), applying only to cases within this regime. Fixed targets are set at 52 months for first-time bankrupts or 76 months for repeat bankrupts, calculated based on statutory criteria involving income, earning ability, and reasonable expenses. The Minister noted that the Ministry of Law regularly reviews the bankruptcy regime to balance debtor and creditor interests. Specific statistics regarding full payment rates, average/median bankruptcy durations, and the adequacy of the calculation formula were not provided in the response.

results.title: Timeline and Scope of Review of Fixed-drop-off Point Scheme for Licensed Cross-border Point-to-point Services between Singapore and Johor Bahru
results.summary: The Acting Minister for Transport stated that the timeline and scope of the Ministry's review of the fixed-drop-off point scheme for licensed cross-border point-to-point services between Singapore and Johor Bahru were addressed in a written reply to Parliamentary Question No 35 at the 23 September 2025 Parliament Sitting. Details regarding discussions with Malaysia on expanding approved drop-off and pick-up points were not provided in this transcript. Further information is available through the referenced written reply.

results.title: Tracking of and Reviewing Legislation for Domestic Child Abduction Cases
results.summary: The Minister for Social and Family Development stated there are no immediate plans to introduce new laws or expedited processes for domestic child abduction cases, citing recent enhancements. Since 1 July 2024, all divorcing parents must attend a mandatory co-parenting programme, supported by interventions like the Children of Divorce Intervention Programme. Effective 2 January 2025, enforcement measures for breached access orders were broadened to include less confrontational options such as make-up access, compensation, and compliance bonds, while simplifying the application process to replace contempt proceedings. The Ministry will continue monitoring the situation to assess if further enhancements are required.

results.title: Transfer of Unused Balances in Child Development Account to Edusave Account Upon Child's Entry into Primary School
results.summary: The Ministry of Education will not transfer unused Child Development Account (CDA) balances to the Edusave Account upon a child's entry into primary school. The Minister explained that CDA funds are designated for preschool, childcare, and healthcare expenses up to age 12, whereas Edusave supports primary and secondary education fees and enrichment programmes. Transferring CDA balances would deprive parents of using these funds for non-educational needs during the primary school years. Instead, parents can utilise both accounts concurrently to cover educational and non-educational expenses. Any remaining CDA balance at age 12 will be automatically transferred to the Post-Secondary Education Account (PSEA) to support future educational costs. The Government will continue reviewing CDA usage to ensure relevance to parents' needs.

results.title: Trends for Singapore's Tax Sources and Strategies to Ensure Long-term Tax Revenue Sustainability
results.summary: Corporate income tax and GST remain Singapore's largest revenue sources, though their growth is not guaranteed due to economic volatility, competitive investment landscapes, and evolving global regimes like BEPS Pillar Two. GST revenue increases from the recent rate hike support medium-term priorities, including rising healthcare costs for an ageing population. The Net Investment Returns Contribution (NIRC) from Reserves currently provides approximately one-fifth of the annual budget. The Government will continue prudent expenditure management and adjust tax policies as necessary to ensure long-term revenue sustainability and a resilient, diversified tax base.

results.title: Trends on Duration to Exit Bankruptcy from 2010 to 2025 and Possible Review of Bankruptcy Laws
results.summary: The Minister reported that median and mean bankruptcy discharge durations declined in the 2020–2025 period, with negligible differences between first-time and repeat bankrupts. The 2015 Differentiated Discharge Framework commenced on 1 August 2016, followed by a 2023 transition where private trustees administer all new bankruptcies except for public interest cases. Concurrently, the Debt Repayment Scheme is under review to curb misuse by debtors influenced by third-party consultants for irresponsible borrowing. The Ministry of Law will continue collaborating with stakeholders to maintain a rehabilitative regime that balances debtor and creditor interests.

results.title: University Students Taking On Multiple Internships Simultaneously
results.summary: Almost all autonomous university courses require an internship for graduation, with credit-bearing placements lasting two weeks to 12 months. The 2024 survey indicates nine in 10 graduates completed at least one internship, averaging roughly two per participant over the last three years. The Ministry of Education and autonomous universities do not track simultaneous or unpaid internships, though institutions report such cases are uncommon. Internship durations vary by institution and course requirements.

results.title: Update on $150 Million Drainage Projects to Improve Flood Resilience in FY2025
results.summary: In FY2025, Singapore will spend approximately $150 million on drainage upgrading projects to enhance flood resilience, having completed four of 19 ongoing initiatives and commenced three of six new projects. Since 2011, the Government has invested about $2.5 billion, averaging $180 million annually, to alleviate flood risks at served locations. The Minister noted that direct comparisons with Tokyo and Hong Kong are not meaningful due to differing weather conditions, topography, and infrastructure requirements. The review for the FY2026 to FY2030 cycle is currently underway to identify key projects for the next five years, with results expected in the coming months.

results.title: Update on Development Plans for Changi Airport Terminal 5
results.summary: Changi Airport Terminal 5 (T5) is scheduled for completion in the mid-2030s, following a groundbreaking ceremony in May 2025 and the awarding of tunnel and substructure contracts in the first half of 2025. Additional tenders for above-ground construction and the ground transportation centre will be issued in subsequent years. The new terminal will connect to Terminal 2 via an underground automated people mover and baggage handling system to enable seamless transfers between terminals.

results.title: Update on Estate Upgrading Programme for Jalan Naung and Realty Park Private Estates
results.summary: The Estate Upgrading Programme for Jalan Naung and Realty Park private estates is targeted to commence by mid-2026, following a tender for consultants by end-2025. Works will include recreational spaces like community gardens and fitness corners, plus connectivity improvements such as footpaths, barrier-free ramps, and wayfinding markers. The Building and Construction Authority will oversee the tender process to appoint the necessary consultants before project design begins.

results.title: Update on Installations of Red-Amber-Green Arrows at Traffic Junctions by Year End, and Potential Elimination of Discretionary Right Turns
results.summary: The Land Transport Authority (LTA) is nearing completion of 200 additional Red-Amber-Green (RAG) arrows, targeting approximately 1,400 installations by end-2025 to cover nearly all signalised junctions island-wide. Remaining junctions with single right-turn lanes and one oncoming lane will not receive arrows unless reviewed during future road developments or upgrades. The Minister did not address the query regarding the viability of eliminating all discretionary right turns by the end of the decade.

results.title: Update on Online Safety Commission to Better Protect Children from Harmful Content
results.summary: The Ministry of Digital Development and Information requires designated app stores to implement age assurance measures by 31 March 2026 to prevent young users under 18 from accessing age-inappropriate apps. Designated social media services must apply stringent content moderation for children and provide parental management tools, with similar age assurance requirements for these platforms expected later this year. The Online Safety (Relief and Accountability) Bill will be tabled in the first half of 2026 to establish the new Online Safety Commission. This dedicated agency will support victims of specified online harms, such as cyberbullying, by issuing directions to stop or mitigate harm. Concurrently, MDDI and the Infocomm Media Development Authority collaborate with the Ministries of Health, Education, and Social and Family Development, along with community organisations, to provide parents with information resources and actionable tips for fostering healthy digital habits.

results.title: Update on Projected Lifespan of Semakau Landfill and Future Waste Management Solutions
results.summary: Semakau Landfill is projected to reach full capacity around 2035 based on current waste disposal rates. The National Environment Agency is exploring long-term solutions, including repurposing waste residues like incineration ash through recovery and processing for land reclamation. These efforts complement existing initiatives to promote recycling and reduce waste generation. The Minister confirmed these measures are being actively investigated to address future waste management needs once the landfill reaches its limit.

results.title: Transport Sector (Miscellaneous Amendments) Bill
results.summary: The Transport Sector (Miscellaneous Amendments) Bill 2025 amends land, sea, and public utility transport legislation to regulate commuter conduct, consolidate security screening powers from the Road Traffic Act 1961 into the Bus Services Industry Act, and update enforcement levers for passenger safety and worker protection. While Mr Ang Wei Neng supports the Bill, he proposes establishing a dedicated COE-PT Fund to offset fare increases and expand Public Transport Voucher eligibility, potentially linking it to CHAS. Conversely, Mr Dennis Tan Lip Fong questions the necessity of expanding mandatory x-ray screening and free searches from the MRT/LRT network to buses, citing operational feasibility, threat profile, and potential delays at co-located interchanges. The amendments also address abuse of transport workers, though specific training protocols for authorised officers and the full scope of the proposed COE-PT Fund remain to be detailed in the debate.

results.title: Update on Statistics for Childminding Pilot Scheme
results.summary: Under the Early Childhood Development Agency's Childminding Pilot Scheme, approximately 60% of enrolled infants are cared for in childminders' homes, while 40% are in two existing community premises. ECDA continues to explore additional community premises to meet demand. Matching families to suitable childminders involves interviews and home visits, with waiting times generally ranging from a few days to a few months depending on service requirements and family preferences.

results.title: Update on Study on Effectiveness of Mandating Age Limits for Social Media Access
results.summary: The Ministry of Digital Development and Information (MDDI) is implementing age assurance measures across app stores to reduce young users' access to age-inappropriate content, extending beyond social media apps. Designated App Distribution Services must implement these measures by 31 March 2026, initially requiring minimal verification that users are under 18. MDDI will subsequently engage designated social media services on similar age assurance requirements later this year, complementing existing protections under the Code of Practice for Online Safety for Social Media Services. Acknowledging that no current solution is foolproof, the Ministry will collaborate with the Ministries of Health, Education, and Social and Family Development, alongside community partners, to provide parents with information resources and actionable tips to support child safety online.

results.title: Usage Statistics by Age Group for Borrow-n-Deliver Pilot Programme and Plans to Further Promote Scheme
results.summary: The National Library Board launched the Borrow-n-Deliver pilot in 2023, fulfilling over 5,000 orders involving more than 11,000 books. Borrower demographics show the largest concentration in the 40 to 49 years age group, followed by 30 to 39 years. To promote greater usage, NLB is exploring collaborations with partners for awareness and has extended a one-time complimentary service for eligible patrons as part of its 30th anniversary celebrations.

results.title: Using Vehicular Dimensions Instead of Vehicular Weight in Defining "Heavy Vehicle" under Vehicle Parking Certificate Scheme
results.summary: The Acting Minister for Transport explained that the Vehicle Parking Certificate (VPC) scheme uses Maximum Laden Weight (MLW) rather than dimensions to define heavy vehicles. This approach prioritizes road safety, as vehicles loaded beyond their designated weight have reduced steering and stopping capabilities, and addresses building owners' concerns regarding carpark structural capacity. The Minister noted that numerous countries, including the United Kingdom and European Union member states, adopt this weight-based classification. Consequently, the Ministry will not switch to using vehicular dimensions for determining VPC requirements.
